narrative: As an Asian male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.60—which is below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 30, lower than the usual 36.70 seen among past students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on similar student data, estimated an 83.6% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high predicted failure risk means the school views you as a student who might struggle to meet the bar's demands right away, despite your full-time commitment, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year—far below the average decile of about 5.5—which strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also a 1 compared to the typical 5.7 average, reinforcing concerns about early academic struggles that often carry over to bar performance. Your family income bracket offered some counterbalance, gently nudging the prediction toward success since upper-middle-class backgrounds correlate with slightly better outcomes on average. Overall, these early-grade deciles painted a picture riskier than most applicants, whose stronger rankings typically predict passing the bar. While your LSAT and GPA contributed to the concern, it was the consistent low deciles that dominated the model's thinking for someone in your position. This doesn't define your potential, especially as an Asian male where many overcome similar hurdles through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above average, strengthening your undergraduate record perhaps through a post-bac program, or gaining relevant experience like internships to demonstrate growth. Reapplying after addressing these core academic flags could shift the prediction in your favor, and schools often value persistence in applicants like you. Keep pushing—you've got the drive to rewrite this story.
